context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on global actors and institutions in development. I’m kinda stuck on how to organize the typology part and connect it to how the development agenda gets shaped.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we think about how to organize a typology, let's build from what you already know. From your readings, who are some of the key global actors you've come across that are involved in development? Just list a few that come to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and asking for organizational help. I should not scaffold too much or give them a structure. Instead, I'll use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what they already know, starting with the typology question since that's what they flagged. I'll ask them what actors they've already identified in their readings to see where they stand before guiding further.

exchanges.student: Umm, maybe the World Bank, IMF, UN agencies, and NGOs? Also like donor states and maybe big corporations too, but idk if those count the same way.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting list. Now here's a key question: what makes the World Bank *different* from, say, an NGO or a multinational corporation? What do you think separates these actors from one another in terms of how they operate or what gives them their power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable initial list. I should validate that they're on the right track without giving them the answer, and push them to think critically about the differences between these actors — which is the core of building a typology. A good Socratic question here would push them to notice what distinguishes these actors from each other, which will naturally lead them toward a typology.

exchanges.student: I think it’s like their source of power? The World Bank has money and policy leverage, NGOs have advocacy/implementation, and corporations have investment and jobs maybe.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking. Now look back at the prompt — it specifically asks about economic, legal, political, *and* cultural resources. You've touched on economic. What about the other three? Can you match any of your actors to those kinds of power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress identifying sources of power as a distinguishing feature. This maps well to the prompt's mention of 'economic, legal, political and cultural resources.' I should push them to connect this insight to the prompt more explicitly — specifically the four types of resources mentioned — without giving them the framework directly.

exchanges.student: Legal could be treaties or loan conditions maybe? Political is probs states and IOs setting priorities, and cultural might be experts/ideas/norms, but I’m not fully sure tbh.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track, especially on the cultural piece — think about who gets to define what 'development' even means. That's a powerful idea worth sitting with. Now, given what you've just described, how do you think the 'development agenda' actually gets shaped? Who has the most influence and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress and getting close to key concepts like conditionality, norm-setting, and epistemic authority. I should affirm the direction without confirming everything, and now push them toward the second half of their problem — how the development agenda actually emerges from these actors. This is a natural pivot that keeps the conversation moving toward the full assignment.

exchanges.student: I guess the agenda gets shaped by the actors w/ the most money, legitimacy, and expertise? So like powerful states, the World Bank/IMF, and maybe UN bodies since they can fund stuff, set rules, and make certain ideas seem like the normal path.
exchanges.tutor: That's a compelling argument. One part of the prompt you haven't addressed yet though — what is the *scale* at which these global institutions operate? What does 'scale' even mean in this context, and why might it matter for your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong core argument connecting resources to agenda-setting power. This is good material for their reflection. Since we're about 5 exchanges in and they have solid raw ideas on typology, resources, and agenda-setting, I should now push them to think about the 'scale' question from the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet. This keeps us moving toward a complete response to all parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’m a little confused on “scale” rn — is it just global vs national vs local? If so, I think it matters bc these actors say they’re global but they act through states, cities, NGOs, and local projects.
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question pretty well. Now push that a bit further — if global actors *claim* to operate at a global scale but work *through* local and national actors, what tensions or problems might that create? And I'd say you now have enough ideas to start drafting. Have you thought about how you'd open your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually answered their own question quite well — they've grasped the multi-scalar nature of global institutions operating through different levels. I should affirm this without over-praising, and nudge them to think about what tensions or implications this creates, which would add analytical depth to their reflection. We've now covered most of the prompt's components, so I should also start nudging them toward writing.

exchanges.student: Maybe a tension is that “global” priorities can override local needs or democratic accountability, even when they’re framed as universal. For the opening, I’m thinking smth like: global institutions don’t just operate at one scale; they shape development by linking transnational power to national policy and local implementation.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you identified is analytically strong — it gets at something real about power and legitimacy. You now have ideas on scale, typology, resources, and agenda-setting. I think it's time to stop discussing and start writing. Try pulling these ideas together into a first draft. What's your plan for structuring the 700-800 words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a strong tension argument and a promising opening idea. However, per the rules, I should not evaluate or polish their language, and I must not write or suggest language for them. The opening they described is their own — I can acknowledge the direction is good without commenting on the wording. Now is a good moment to push them toward actually writing, since we've covered all the main components of the prompt. I should ask them to integrate their ideas into a draft rather than continuing to discuss in the abstract.

exchanges.student: I think intro on scale + thesis, then a para on typology, then a para on the resources they use, then one on how the agenda gets set, and a short conclusion. Does that sound too basic, or is that okay for this kind of reflection?
exchanges.tutor: That's not a question I can answer for you — only your instructor can say what's expected. What I'd ask instead is: does your structure actually respond to *all four* questions in the prompt? Check them against your outline and see if anything is missing or underdeveloped. Then start writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their structure, which risks becoming dependent on my approval rather than their own judgment. I should redirect them to think critically about it themselves, and also gently remind them that I can't grade or approve their outline. The more important nudge is to get them actually writing rather than seeking validation on the structure.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think it hits all four, but maybe the “from where” part of the resources question is still weak. Can u help me figure out what “from where” means there?
exchanges.tutor: Good catch. Think about it this way — when the World Bank funds a project, where does that money actually come from? And when the IMF sets conditions on a loan, whose interests and ideas are behind those conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine gap in their thinking about the 'from where' part of the resources question. This is good self-awareness. I should use a Socratic question to help them figure it out themselves rather than explaining it. A simple question about the origins or sources of these resources should do it.

exchanges.student: Ohhh, so “from where” means the origins of their power/resources — like member state contributions, voting power of rich countries, international law, and expert knowledge from elite institutions. I think I can use that, but I’m still not sure if NGOs fit in the same typology as states/IOs.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the 'from where' — that's a meaningful addition to your argument. On NGOs: you've raised this a few times now. Rather than resolving it in the abstract, make a decision, justify it briefly in your draft, and move on. What matters is your reasoning, not a perfect categorization. Time to write — you have everything you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has had a good realization about 'from where' and should be acknowledged briefly. On the NGO question, they've been circling this concern a couple of times now. Rather than continuing to discuss it abstractly, I should push them to make a decision and start writing. We're at exchange 10, so I need to wrap the conversation toward drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. No direct answers or submission-ready content were provided; instead, the tutor consistently prompted the student to generate ideas, identify tensions, and make their own decisions. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student response before advancing. Responses were concise and appropriately bite-sized. Tone was supportive without being effusive, and the tutor maintained a clear formative coaching stance.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. On 1.1, the tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready content; every substantive idea (typology, resources, agenda-setting, scale tensions) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. On 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'You just answered your own question pretty well') and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., asking what 'scale' means before elaborating). On 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback, notably redirecting the student to check their outline against all four prompt questions rather than just validating the structure. Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation advanced progressively without repetitive cycling, and the tutor kept the student anchored to the prompt throughout. Communication was concise and clear, with no monologues, and the tone was appropriately supportive and non-evaluative.
